perturbation.original_text: Officials, employees and agents of Authorized Person having access to any confidential information obtained pursuant to this agreement shall be subjec t to all federal and state laws, and regulations , and will comply with limitations on use, treatment, and safeguarding of data under the following laws, regulations and agreements : K.S.A. 44- 714(e) , and amendments thereto ; the Privacy Act of 1974 (5 U.S.C. 552a), as amended by the Computer Matching and Privacy Protection Act of 1988, related Office of Management and Budget guidelines, the Federal Information Security Management Act of 2002 (44 U.S.C. § 3541, et seq.), and related National Institute of Standards and Technology guidelines ; SSA’s “Electronic Information Exchange Security Requirements and Procedures for State and Local Agencies Exchanging Electronic Information with the Social Security Administration ” (TSSR v7) ; the US Department of Health and Human Services requirements relating to National Directory of New Hires data ; and the Information Exchange Agreement between the Social Security Administration and the State of Kansas Department of Labor .
perturbation.changed_text: Officials, employees and agents of Authorized Person having access to any confidential information obtained pursuant to this agreement should endeavor to be subject to applicable federal and state laws and regulations. They will strive to comply with limitations on use, treatment, and safeguarding of data under the following laws, regulations, and agreements: K.S.A. 44-714(e), and amendments thereto; the Privacy Act of 1974 (5 U.S.C. 552a), as amended by the Computer Matching and Privacy Protection Act of 1988, related Office of Management and Budget guidelines; the Federal Information Security Management Act of 2002 (44 U.S.C. § 3541, et seq.), and related National Institute of Standards and Technology guidelines; SSA’s “Electronic Information Exchange Security Requirements and Procedures for State and Local Agencies Exchanging Electronic Information with the Social Security Administration” (TSSR v7); the US Department of Health and Human Services requirements relating to National Directory of New Hires data; and the Information Exchange Agreement between the Social Security Administration and the State of Kansas Department of Labor.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be subject to' to 'should endeavor to be subject to' weakens the obligation to comply with laws and regulations. Replacing 'will comply' with 'will strive to comply' introduces ambiguity, suggesting that full compliance is not mandatory. This weakens the enforceability of data protection requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: K.S.A. 44-714(e) mandates that information obtained in the administration of employment security laws must be held confidential and used solely for purposes consistent with those laws. The modified text's weakening of the compliance obligation with 'should endeavor' and 'strive to comply' undermines the strict confidentiality and usage requirements established by this statute, potentially leading to violations if Authorized Persons do not fully comply.

Previous Next 44-714. Administration of act; powers and duties of secretary; employees, agents and contractors; reports and records, confidentiality; disclosure of information; witnesses, oaths and subpoenas; state-federal cooperation; fees for document copies. (a) Duties and powers of secretary. It shall be the duty of the secretary to administer this act and the secretary shall have power and authority to adopt, amend or revoke such rules and regulations, to employ such persons, make such expenditures, require such reports, make such investigations, and take such other action as the secretary deems necessary or suitable to that end. Such rules and regulations may be adopted, amended, or revoked by the secretary only after public hearing or opportunity to be heard thereon. The secretary shall determine the organization and methods of procedure in accordance with the provisions of this act, and shall have an official seal which shall be judicially noticed. The secreta
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall be subject to' and 'will comply' to 'should endeavor to be subject to' and 'will strive to comply' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to comply with data protection laws and regulations. The modified text undermines the mandatory nature of K.S.A. 44-714(e), which requires that information be held confidential and used solely for purposes consistent with employment security laws. The snippet provided states that the secretary has the duty to administer the act, implying a strict adherence to the law, which is contradicted by the changes.

perturbation.original_text: Authorized Person agrees that they will not provide or allow access of the information obtained hereunder to any agent, third party, contractor, subcontractor, or any other public agency, unless authorized in writing by KDOL, and then conditioned upon said agent, third party, contractor, s ubcontractor, or any other public agency agreeing in writing to be bound by the use and confidentiality terms and conditions of this Confidentiality Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Authorized Person agrees that they will use reasonable judgment in deciding whether to provide or allow access of the information obtained hereunder to any agent, third party, contractor, subcontractor, or any other public agency, unless authorized in writing by KDOL, and preferably conditioned upon said agent, third party, contractor, subcontractor, or any other public agency agreeing in writing to be bound by the use and confidentiality terms and conditions of this Confidentiality Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The insertion of 'reasonable judgment' makes the prohibition of unauthorized access less strict, allowing for subjective interpretation. Changing 'conditioned upon' to 'preferably conditioned upon' makes the requirement for third-party agreements non-mandatory, weakening the protection of confidential information. This introduces ambiguity in ensuring third party compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: K.S.A. 44-714(e) requires strict confidentiality. By allowing Authorized Persons to use 'reasonable judgment' and weakening the requirement for written agreements with third parties, the modified text increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure, contradicting the statute's mandate for confidentiality.

Administration of act; powers and duties of secretary; employees, agents and contractors; reports and records, confidentiality; disclosure of information; witnesses, oaths and subpoenas; state-federal cooperation; fees for document copies. (a) Duties and powers of secretary. It shall be the duty of the secretary to administer this act and the secretary shall have power and authority to adopt, amend or revoke such rules and regulations, to employ such persons, make such expenditures, require such reports, make such investigations, and take such other action as the secretary deems necessary or suitable to that end. Such rules and regulations may be adopted, amended, or revoked by the secretary only after public hearing or opportunity to be heard thereon. The secretary shall determine the organization and methods of procedure in accordance with the provisions of this act, and shall have an official seal which shall be judicially noticed. The secreta
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates adherence to confidentiality agreements for third parties accessing information, whereas the changed contract introduces 'reasonable judgment' and makes third-party agreements 'preferably conditioned upon,' weakening confidentiality. The Law Explanation states that K.S.A. 44-714(e) requires strict confidentiality. The modified text increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure, contradicting the statute’s mandate for confidentiality. Scraped Law Snippet 2, citing Kansas Statutes § 44-714, relates to the administration of the act and powers of the secretary, including reports and records confidentiality and disclosure of information. Although the specific subsection (e) is not present in the snippet, the reference to 44-714 and 'confidentiality; disclosure of information' provides context supporting the claim that the changed text contradicts legal confidentiality requirements by introducing ambiguity.

perturbation.original_text: Authorized Person agrees to comply with all data storage requirements in IRS Publication 1075 for a ll information systems used for receiving, processing, storing, or transmitting confidential information obtained pursuant to this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Authorized Person agrees to make good faith efforts to follow data storage guidelines similar to those in IRS Publication 1075 for information systems used for handling confidential information obtained pursuant to this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'comply with all data storage requirements' to 'make good faith efforts to follow data storage guidelines similar to' significantly weakens the obligation. It replaces a strict mandate with a subjective standard, and specifies 'guidelines similar to' opening the door to alternative interpretations rather than following the strict rules.
perturbation.law_explanation: IRS Publication 1075 sets forth mandatory security standards to protect federal tax information (FTI). The modified text replaces the explicit requirement to comply with Publication 1075 with a vague obligation to make 'good faith efforts' to follow 'similar' guidelines. This weakens the security standards and increases the risk of unauthorized access or disclosure of FTI, thus contradicting the rigorous security requirements of IRS Publication 1075.

perturbation.justification: The original text requires compliance with IRS Publication 1075, a strict mandate. The changed text weakens this to 'good faith efforts to follow data storage guidelines similar to' the publication. The Law Explanation states that this 'weakens the security standards and increases the risk of unauthorized access or disclosure of FTI, thus contradicting the rigorous security requirements of IRS Publication 1075.' While the scraped snippets don't directly state the specific data requirements, Snippet 2 discusses the Inspector General's role in oversight, promoting integrity and efficiency and helping detect fraud, waste, and abuse within the IRS, which is undermined by weakening data protection standards.

perturbation.original_text: Both parties shall exercise reas onable and prudent security procedures to protect such information, reports, returns and other documentation in their possession, including electronic versions thereof, from any unauthorized access and/or disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties may exercise security procedures deemed reasonable by their respective IT departments to protect such information, reports, returns and other documentation in their possession, including electronic versions thereof, from known unauthorized access and/or disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall exercise reasonable and prudent security procedures' with 'may exercise security procedures deemed reasonable by their respective IT departments' makes the implementation of security measures discretionary and reliant on internal IT judgment. Adding 'known' before 'unauthorized access' limits the protection to only known threats, neglecting unknown or emerging threats. This weakens the overall security posture and increases the risk of breaches.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Federal Information Security Management Act of 2002 (FISMA) (44 U.S.C. § 3541, et seq.)
perturbation.law_explanation: FISMA requires federal agencies and their contractors to develop, document, and implement an information security program to protect federal information and information systems. By making security procedures discretionary and limiting protection to 'known' unauthorized access, the modified text fails to meet the comprehensive security requirements mandated by FISMA, increasing the vulnerability of confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates 'reasonable and prudent security procedures,' while the changed text allows for security procedures 'deemed reasonable by their respective IT departments' and limits protection to 'known' unauthorized access. This introduces ambiguity and discretion where the original contract clearly required specific security measures. The explanation provided states that FISMA requires federal agencies and their contractors to develop, document, and implement an information security program to protect federal information and information systems, which this updated text does not satisfy. The first snippet makes references to Title 44, Chapter 35, Subchapter III, Confidential Information Protection. This, alongside the explanation stating the updated text 'fails to meet the comprehensive security requirements mandated by FISMA' indicates a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Once confidential information or data obtained hereunder is no longer useful for the purposes described herein, Authorized Person agrees to immediately destroy all such confidential information or data maintained on any information system platform or any form of storage media subject to any schedule of retention established by the Kansas Records Board , in the following manner:
perturbation.changed_text: When confidential information or data obtained hereunder is deemed no longer essential for the purposes described herein, Authorized Person will endeavor to destroy such confidential information or data maintained on information systems or storage media, considering any schedule of retention established by the Kansas Records Board, as feasible:
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'is no longer useful' to 'is deemed no longer essential' allows for subjective interpretation of when data should be destroyed. Replacing 'agrees to immediately destroy' with 'will endeavor to destroy' makes data destruction non-mandatory and weakens the timeline. Changing 'subject to any schedule of retention' to 'considering any schedule of retention' makes following Kansas Records Board guidelines optional, increasing the risk of non-compliance with state record retention policies.
perturbation.law_explanation: K.S.A. 45-404 requires compliance with records retention schedules established by the Kansas Records Board. By making data destruction non-mandatory and weakening the adherence to the retention schedule, the modified text contradicts the statute's mandate for compliance with records retention policies, potentially leading to unauthorized retention or destruction of state records.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Kansas Statutes § 45-404 (2023) - Duties of state records board; disposition of county records. :: 2023 Kansas Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Kansas Stat. 2023 Kansas Stat. (here) 2021 Kansas Stat. View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Kansas Statutes Chapter 45 - Public Records, Documents And Information Article 4 - Public Records Preservation 45-404 Duties of state records board; disposition of county records. Universal Citation: KS Stat § 45-404 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 45-404. Duties of state records board; disposition of county records. The state records board shall: (a) Approve or modify retention and disposition schedules and records manuals prepared pursuant to subsections (c) and (d) of K.S.A. 45-406 and amendments thereto. Once approved by the board the retention and disposition schedules for state agencies shall be filed with the secretary of state. Without further action by the board, noncurrent records of state agencies scheduled for disposition may be disposed of as provided in the schedules and noncurrent records scheduled for retention may be transferred to the state archives, subject to approval by the state archivist and in accordance with procedures to be established by the state archivist. (b) Pass upon any proposed revisions in the retention and disposition schedules and upon requests for authority to dispose of records of state agencies or counties not listed in the schedules. No records of state agencies or counties shall be disposed of before the retention periods designated in the schedules have elapsed without the approval of the board. No state agency or county shall be required to destroy records which it chooses to retain, even though the retention and disposition schedules authorize their destruction. The retention an
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts K.S.A. 45-404 by weakening the mandatory compliance with records retention schedules. The original text states an agreement to 'immediately destroy' information subject to the Kansas Records Board schedule, while the changed text uses 'will endeavor to destroy' and 'considering any schedule of retention', making adherence optional. This directly undermines the duty of the state records board as outlined in Kansas Statutes § 45-404, which states, 'No records of state agencies or counties shall be disposed of before the retention periods designated in the schedules have elapsed without the approval of the board'. The changes introduce ambiguity and discretion where the statute intends a mandatory compliance, creating a legal contradiction.
